Patch Set 1: Verified

Build Successful 
 
http://build.whamcloud.com/job/lustre-reviews/19584/ : SUCCESS

Patch Set 1: Looks good to me, but someone else must approve

With patched lfs and unpatched 3.12.0-rc6 kernel, I got:
[Fedora16@~]$sudo ./lfs setstripe -c 2 /mnt/lustre/haha/tt
error on ioctl 0x4008669a for '/mnt/lustre/haha/tt' (3): stripe already set
error: setstripe: create stripe file '/mnt/lustre/haha/tt' failed

After patching client kernel with the new O_LOV_DELAY_CREATE definition, lfs setstripe works.

Since O_LOV_DELAY_CREATE is broken with old definition anyway, the patch looks good to me.

Thanks for helping solving the problem!
Patch Set 1: Looks good to me, but someone else must approve

Patch Set 2: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

(2 inline comments)

Bobijam, could you please explain why this patch works and the previous one (which only checks if only a single flag is set) fails?  Is the kernel setting some of these flags internally?

The use of 020000000 by itself has never existed, so I think the only valid values passed from userspace would be 0100000000 (pre-2.4 clients), 0120000000 (2.4-2.5 clients), and (0120000000 | O_NOCTTY) (2.6+ clients).  Eventually, this should change to O_NOCTTY (3.x clients, or earlier?)

Patch Set 2:

Tao, when submitting this patch upstream, I'm wondering if Greg will object to this patch?  Should we change the commit message so it is less objectionable?

Patch Set 2:

Tests received by maloo, run on CentOS release 6.4/x86_64: (https://maloo.whamcloud.com/test_sessions/ebb2d036-5953-11e3-8e2b-52540035b04c).  Ran 19 tests.  1 tests failed: sanity.

Patch Set 2:

I observed that the previous test failure due to that VFS passes O_NOCTTY (0104501) down to ll_file_open() even when it tries to create a file.

Patch Set 2:

Do you know where the O_NOCTTY flag is coming from?  Is it glibc or something? I don't see it in the kernel. I guess with flags 0104501 then none of the other old flags are Laing it through the VFS. 

The only other idea I have is for setstripe to create a file with mknod() and then setxattr() on the file before opening it. This increases the RPC count but avoids the need for a special flag.

Patch Set 2:

strace touch /mnt/lustre/file 

shows


...
open("/mnt/lustre/file", O_WRONLY|O_CREAT|O_NOCTTY|O_NONBLOCK, 0666) = 3
...

Patch Set 2:

"echo string > lustre_file" or "cat oldfile > lustre_file" would call ll_file_open() with flags as 0101101

Patch Set 2:

Any ideas on how to solve this problem cleanly?

We could check for the absence of O_NODELAY, but that isn't very robust if O_NOCTTY is used by some other application. I figured O_NOCTTY would only be used by applications that know they are opening a tty device, but I guess it is also used by apps that might open random files and want to be safe in case they do open a tty device. 

A similar mechanism would be to check a combination of flags, but I don't see another one that is obvious. I looked at FASYNC, but it doesn't look as safe as I'd like. 

A second option is to fall back to creating the objects with a default layout if no ioctl() is called. I sort of thought that was the default behaviour in the past, but I haven't looked at the details for a long time.

There is also the option of actually trying to get a reserved flag in the upstream kernel, but I expect a reply like "don't do that, system calls are cheap" that isn't really helpful.

My preference at this point is the second option of creating objects with the default layout (as if LL_IOC_SETSTRIPE was called with all default options) if the file does anything on the client.

Patch Set 2:

then it'll change the client behavior, and would be incompatible with old client again, is it?

Patch Set 2:

(1 comment)

I'm working on a new version of this patch.
Uploaded patch set 3.

I tested the following statically-linked lfs binaries worked properly ("lfs setstripe -c 3 /lustre/file" + "lfs getstripe -c /lustre/file" to verify it worked):

 lfsver    lustrever
 ------    ---------
 patched   patched
 2.4.2     patched
 patched   2.4.2
 2.1.3     patched

The main question now is whether we think this is a suitable long-term fix, or too much of a hack to live?

Patch Set 7:

I'd also be obliged if someone could test this patch on a new kernel where O_TMPFILE exists and breaks the current lfs.

the only kernel I have access to right now with O_TMPFILE in it is the 3.12 kernel in fc19/20. unfortunately I can't even build lustre clients on that kernel due to problems listed in LU-4416.

Patch Set 8: Code-Review+1

I confirmed this is working with O_TMPFILE flag in newer kernel. Just not sure if it is acceptable in upstream kernel.

[Fedora16@~]$sudo ./lfs setstripe -c 2 /mnt/lustre/t1
[Fedora16@~]$sudo ./lfs getstripe /mnt/lustre/t1
/mnt/lustre/t1
lmm_stripe_count:   2
lmm_stripe_size:    1048576
lmm_pattern:        1
lmm_layout_gen:     0
lmm_stripe_offset:  0
        obdidx           objid           objid           group
             0               2            0x2                0
             1               2            0x2                0

[Fedora16@~]$uname -r
3.13.0-rc5+
Patch Set 8: Code-Review+1

Patch Set 8:

Using just O_LOV_DELAY_CREATE_MASK isn't so great, since it means that the same lustre-tests RPM cannot be used for testing the upstream kernel and the regular kernel. It probably makes more sense to change the test tools to use liblustreapi instead if hard-coding the ioctl anyway.

Patch Set 8:

While we can change the test tools, that does not fix an other tools potentially out there.

So I think we should just have O_LOV_DELAY_CREATE to always represent the current mask.

We definitely should just drop the 0100000000 flag completely and never ever remembering about it.

Old lustre versions don't care about it, in-kernel client was not too good when it was in use and still functional, so nobody could be expected to use it.
And old tools always shipped it with 020000000 as well, making it not needed.

Hm, I imagine since 020000000 is just dropped by the kernel, it's no big deal to actually combine 020000000 and O_LOV_DELAY_CREATE_MASK to get O_LOV_DELAY_CREATE if we really want to, but that's about it.
Patch Set 8: Code-Review+1
Uploaded patch set 9.

parents.subject: LU-4554 lfsck: old single-OI MDT always scrubbed

commit.subject: LU-4209 utils: fix O_TMPFILE/O_LOV_DELAY_CREATE conflict
commit.message:
LU-4209 utils: fix O_TMPFILE/O_LOV_DELAY_CREATE conflict

In kernel 3.11 O_TMPFILE was introduced, but the open flag value
conflicts with the O_LOV_DELAY_CREATE flag 020000000 added to fix
LU-812 in Lustre 2.4.  O_LOV_DELAY_CREATE allows applications
to defer file layout and object creation from open time (the default)
until it can instead be specified by the application using an ioctl.

Instead of trying to find a non-conflicting O_LOV_DELAY_CREATE flag
or define a Lustre-specific flag that isn't of use to most/any other
filesystems, use (O_NOCTTY|FASYNC) as the new value.  These flags
are not meaningful for newly-created regular files and should be
ok since O_LOV_DELAY_CREATE is only meaningful for new files.

I looked into using O_ACCMODE/FMODE_WRITE_IOCTL, which allows calling
ioctl() on the minimally-opened fd and is close to what is needed,
but that doesn't allow specifying the actual read or write mode for
the file, and fcntl(F_SETFL) doesn't allow O_RDONLY/O_WRONLY/O_RDWR
to be set after the file is opened.

We will keep the 0100000000 flag for backward compatibility until
3.13 is the oldest client kernel that is supported, but drop the
conflicting __O_TMPFILE value of 02000000 since that will cause an
error when running on newer kernels.  The 020000000 has only been
used since Lustre 2.4.0 and always in conjunction with 0100000000,
so any apps that used O_LOV_DELAY_CREATE directly instead of calling
llapi_file_create*() will still work until Linux 3.13 is used.

Signed-off-by: Andreas Dilger <andreas.dilger@intel.com>
Change-Id: I565f3454616edc60c6acee01034aa5d773500c1e
Reviewed-on: http://review.whamcloud.com/8312
Tested-by: Jenkins
Tested-by: Maloo <hpdd-maloo@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Bob Glossman <bob.glossman@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Peng Tao <bergwolf@gmail.com>
Reviewed-by: Oleg Drokin <oleg.drokin@intel.com>
